text: The surgeon inspects the patient's abdomen and observes the small bowel protruding from beneath the Roux limb of the gastric bypass.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical structures involved and begins the process of reducing the hernia, retracting the small bowel that is protruding through the Roux limb.
The video shows the Peterson defect, an area between the mesentery of the large bowel, the mesentery of the small bowel, and the retroperitoneum, where the internal hernia occurs.
After reducing the small bowel from the hernia, the surgeon uses an endoscopic suturing device to close the Peterson defect, ensuring the mesenteric tissues are securely approximated.
The surgeon initiates a purse-string suture at the apex of the hernia defect, then continues with a continuous running suture extending to the border of the transverse colon.
